I always say that people don't have time to fall in love in the city. Now what could be nicer than just spending months and months getting acquainted-- with somebody, of course, that you like--and maybe--could fall in love with?
She rolls over and looks pensively at David.
(hopelessly) Of course it's just possible that he didn't bring it outdoors at all.
You're not even willing to relax for a minute, are you?
(looking around at her bitterly) Relax? Do you realize that I've telephoned my fiancee twice and postponed my marriage?
Yes. That's too bad. It must be terrible for a girl to realize that a bone is more important than she is.
(violently) I haven't told her I've lost the intercostal clavicle! I haven't dared! It would be too great a shock to her!
Oh, I wouldn't tell her I'd lost it, David---I'd just tell her it's been mislaid.
I'm sure you would.
Now I would suggest that you tell her that you'll be married next week at the same time. That will give you a week here. We're sure to find it in a week.
Oh, Susan, stop suggesting things!
(hurt) All right, David. I was only trying to help.
They sit bolt upright as the scream of the panther is heard from the stable. Susan jumps up.
Baby! We haven't fed him!
(bitterly) If it weren't for my fossil, I'd feed him George.
(frantically as another scream is heard) If Aunt Elizabeth hears that-- my goose is cooked!
She plunges off toward the stable and David follows.
131 INT. VANCE STABLE - LATE AFTERNOON

Inside Box Stall -- David and Susan enter, leaving the door open behind them. Susan pats the panther while David unwraps the package of meat he bought at Westlake, which he has taken from the stolen car. As Baby crouches over the meat there is a low growl from the doorway. George has escaped the stern eye of Aunt Elizabeth and is spying as usual.
It's George! Grab him!
You grab him. You know him better.
That's why I won't grab him.
(advancing on George) Keep that panther back. We can't lose George--yet.
Mark said he likes dogs.
Mark probably meant he eats dogs.
He pounces on George in the dim light and goes out of the box stall, Susan following.
132 OUTSIDE BOX STALL
-- David is hugging George firmly as Susan latches the door of the stall on the panther.
He must like you. He hasn't growled since you picked him up.
(bitterly) He can't. His mouth is full of my hand.
133 INT. GUEST BEDROOM - NIGHT

David has changed back into his own clothes except for his coat. He has on a fresh shirt and looks quite respectable again. He is on the telephone in the midst of an agitated conversation with Miss Swallow, his agitation arising largely from Susan's jerking at his elbow.
But, Alice dear, I can't get in! I've been unavoidably detained!
(jerking at his elbow) Tell her at the same time next week.
(putting his hand over the mouthpiece) Susan, will you go away?
(tugging at his elbow) But, David, it might take a week.
(into phone) What, dear? What did you say? ...Yes, dear, I know it's unlike me.
There is a blast from the other end of the wire.
(jerking at his sleeve) Don't be afraid of her, David. Say something funny.
(goaded beyond control, fairly shouts into phone as the blast ceases) I might as well tell you, Alice, --I've lost the intercostal clavicle!
There is a piercing scream from the receiver and he turns it away from his ear with an agonized expression. Susan jerks the receiver from his hand, puts it in the drawer and shuts the drawer.
(aghast as he indicates the drawer) But you can't just leave Alice there!
Who can't?
David jerks open the drawer and takes out the receiver. He speaks tensely into the transmitter.
Alice! Answer me! Alice!
Susan crowds her face into the transmitter and speaks in the crisp manner of an operator.
When you hear the signal, the time will be exactly seven thirty-one and a half.
And she picks up a paper cutter and smacks a brass bowl, which gives out a loud gonglike note.
134 INT. ALICE'S HOME - NIGHT
Alice in a dead faint at the other end of the wire. [Scene Details]
135 INT. ENTRY - VANCE HOUSE - NIGHT

MED. SHOT on front door. The doorbell is ringing. Mrs. Gogarty opens the door to disclose Major Horace Applegate, a well-dressed man of forty-five or fifty. In the driveway, through the door, we see a taxi drawing out of scene.
I am Major Applegate. Mrs. Random asked me for dinner. Will you find out if she still wants me?
Aunt Elizabeth peers over Mrs. Gogarty's shoulder.
Is that you, Horace? Come in.
(entering) Well, well, well, Elizabeth! How are you?
(as CAMERA FOLLOWS them into the living room) I'm a nervous wreck, as you very well know. I'm always a nervous wreck.
What I need is absolute peace and quiet and I never get it. It seems to me all my life I've been searching for absolute peace and quiet and I've
They run into Susan, who comes out of the guest bedroom, where she has been helping David telephone.
Susan, I want you to meet a very dear friend of mine--Major Applegate. (as Susan offers her hand) He wants me to finance a most thrilling expedition and he's invited me to go along.
I don't approve of expeditions. How do you do, Major Applegate?
That needn't bother you. You're not going.
I don't think you should go either, Aunt Elizabeth. (to Applegate) She's really in very delicate health.
(with a nervous laugh) Oh--really, Elizabeth? You-- you didn't tell me that.
(with a snort) I'm no such thing! I'm in perfect health--except that I'm a nervous wreck.
David, a bit ill at ease, enters from the guest bedroom. He stops short on finding himself confronting a stranger -- Applegate. Aunt Elizabeth looks at them.
I want you to meet Mr. Bone.
(extending their hands and speaking simultaneously) How do you do, Mr. Bone!
(losing patience with his paranoia, points a finger at David) You are Mr. Bone. This is Major Applegate.
(laughing uneasily) Oh yes. Yes. Quite. I'm Applegate.
But there must be some mistake -AUNT ELIZABETH Oh no, there isn't! I've known Horace Applegate for twenty years! Susan, take him away!
(with the sweet manner of a keeper) Come, Mr. Bone. We'll go for a nice little walk before dinner.
David goes out in an utter daze. Applegate looks after him.
What a pity! Such a nice-looking young man.

Conversation may be a little difficult at dinner, Horace. The young man's having a nervous breakdown. Talk to him about big game hunting. He's a big game hunter, too. He can't talk sensibly about anything else.
Oh yes, Elizabeth. I can talk for days on that subject. Don't worry. You'll find conversation flowing very freely once I start drawing him out.
136 INT. VANCE STABLE - NIGHT

Gogarty enters like a man who is in search of a bottle, which he is, goes to the door of the box stall where Baby is, unlatches it and enters. He walks past Baby in the dim light, reaches up to a rafter and finds his bottle, goes out with the bottle, leaving the door unlatched. Gogarty sits down on a box and uncorks his bottle. He takes a swig to steady his nerves.
137 INT. VANCE DINING ROOM - NIGHT

Aunt Elizabeth, Major Applegate, David and Susan are seated at dinner. Conversation is slightly impeded because David will not look at anybody but George, who lies on the floor well within view. Mrs. Gogarty has already served soup to David, Susan and Aunt Elizabeth. Now she enters through pantry door, bears down upon Major Applegate.
(clearing his throat as she places the soup before him) Have you ever been in Arabia, Mr. Bone?
Mrs. Gogarty marches back to the kitchen. David, engrossed in George, makes no response and Applegate once more clears his throat as he tries again.
I say, Mr. Bone, have you ever been -- in Arabia?
(abruptly, without taking his eyes off George) No.
(dogged) I suppose you've -- spent most of your time in Africa?
No. Why should I?
Applegate looks helplessly at Aunt Elizabeth, who makes a gesture of washing her hands of the whole business.
Tibet?
No.
(desperately) Malay Peninsula?
George gets up and wanders in a leisurely fashion through the doorway. David rises abruptly.
Excuse me.
Applegate follows David's exit with his gaze.
I think I hit it.
(with a snort) Do you think it's worth it?
(quickly) He was horribly clawed by a tiger in the Malay States. He doesn't like talking about it.
Oh, I see... Don't worry, Elizabeth. I'll draw him out.
Through another door George comes into the room, David stalking after him. George flops down. David resumes his seat and his soup, still keeping his eyes fixed on George.
(tipping David off) I've just been telling Major Applegate that you were badly clawed in the Malay Peninsula.
I've never been in the Malay Peninsula. This throws them all for a loss.
What type of gun do you use in hunting tigers? Again George is on the loose. David rises.
(muttering) Excuse me.
All watch as David stalks out after George.
Really, Susan, imagine giving a dinner party, with your husband stalking
like Hamlet's father all through the meal! It's unthinkable!
(dreamily) Isn't he sweet! Did you ever see such shoulders!
138 INT. VANCE STABLE - NIGHT

Gogarty seated on the box and taking another drink. Baby has come out of the stall and is padding silently toward him. The panther rubs lightly against the nice man. Gogarty reaches down carelessly behind him and pats Baby.
Hello, George, me boyo!

A funny expression comes over his face as he goes on patting Baby. He begins to suspect this isn't a dog, because dogs don't purr. He screws up his courage and looks around, then springs to his feet with an inarticulate cry and rushes out of the stable.
139 INT. VANCE KITCHEN - NIGHT
Mrs. Gogarty has the fish course set out on a silver tray and is just passing the kitchen door as Gogarty bursts in and sends the tray flying. Mrs. Gogarty stares at him indignantly.
Blast you, Gogarty!
140 INT. VANCE DINING ROOM - NIGHT
GROUP SHOT. As David and George enter there is a terrific crash offscene. David, without a word of explanation about his absence, resumes his seat and fixes his eyes on George.
(ringing the bell violently) What was that? Hannah! Hannah!
141 INT. VANCE KITCHEN - NIGHT
TWO SHOT -- Hannah and Gogarty.
Hannah, this is no time for personalities. Where's my gun? MRS. GOGARTY Gun? What would you be wanting with a gun?
Don't be asking questions in a crisis! Where's my gun?
It's where you left it -- in your room under the bed, the night you thought you heard a burglar. What do you want it for now?
A cat it was! (gesturing) That big! With eyes like balls of fire!
The ringing of the dining room bell is heard off-scene, and Aunt Elizabeth's voice. AUNT ELIZABETH'S VOICE
142 INT. VANCE DINING ROOM - NIGHT
MED. SHOT. Mrs. Gogarty appears in the pantry doorway.
Yes, ma'am.
What was all that banging about out there? MRS. GOGARTY
That was Gogarty, ma'am. Gogarty's face appears in the doorway behind her.
I saw a cat! I saw a cat as big as a cow, Mrs. Random! It came right up and breathed in my face!
What nonsense!
That's what I said, ma'am.
Susan and David freeze and lean forward. For the first time David unfixes his gaze from George.
What -- did -- you -- say -- you -- saw?
A cat -- with eyes like balls of fire.
Gogarty, you've been drinking again!
Nary a drop have I had, Mrs. Random, as I live an' breathe. A gun is what's needed and a man to wield it -- an' Gogarty's the man!
Mrs. Gogarty pushes Gogarty back through the door and closes it.
(to ease the social tension; laughing nervously) I saw a tiger once -- in Abyssinia.
(rising as one) Excuse me!
Applegate, checked in mid-sentence, stares open-mouthed at the receding figures.
I suppose it's too much to expect -- but would you mind staying through the meal?
No, no, Elizabeth, I -- er -- I was planning to.
Good! Hannah, serve the next course!
143 INT. VANCE STABLE - NIGHT
Susan and David rush in. The stall is empty.
Baby! BABY!
Now don't lose your head, Susan! Don't lose your head!
(insanely) Don't lose my what?
Don't lose your head!
(clasping her head frantically) I haven't lost my head! I haven't!
(shaking her) Collect yourself, Susan! Nothing is to be gained by uncontrolled hysteria. Stop shaking!
I -- I'm not -- s-shaking. You -- you're -- s-shaking me.
He drops his hands. For the first time Susan has met her Waterloo. She is willing to turn to David for a solution.
144 INT. VANCE DINING ROOM - NIGHT
Mrs. Gogarty enters from the pantry with a platter of roast squab.
(helping herself) One reason why I'd like to go on this expedition, Horace, is that it
would make Drusilla Voorhies green with jealousy. She's always bragging
Oh! Was he -- primitive?
Susan enters and slides into her seat behind Mrs. Gogarty
Oh. Squab.

Ten years, off and on, I've lived with Drusilla -- and I've never seen her stop acting. She can't forget that she once sang at La Scala and she neglects to mention that it cost her third husband ten thousand dollars. A voice like a crow -- and I can't give a party that she doesn't sing after dinner!
Elizabeth, I shouldn't live with her.
But, Horace, she's my dearest friend.
(choking on his food) Oh.
And now that she has that moth-eaten cheetah she's insufferable.
Oh. A cheetah. You mean -(making his four fingers walk on the tablecloth)
Oh, it's perfectly safe. Its teeth are all gone. But it walks, and crowds follow her -- and that's all she wants. Attention! That's why I left Paris. I will not be put in the shade by that woman!
145 INT. GUEST BEDROOM - NIGHT
David at telephone in an agitated state.
Listen! It doesn't matter what my name is. I -- saw -- a -- panther!
146 INT. ZOO OFFICE - NIGHT
CLOSE SHOT -- Zoo official in uniform, at phone.
Yeah, I heard you. You saw a panther. (drily) In Connecticut!
147 INT. VANCE DINING ROOM - NIGHT
Susan, Applegate, Aunt Elizabeth at table. Susan has finished her squab. She is prattling to cover David's telephoning.
I know just how you feel, Aunt Elizabeth, about the Voorhies woman. Cheetahs are nasty things to have around the house. Any big animal is a lot of bother.
(brusquely) Nothing of the kind! I'm going to have a panther. Mark's sending it from Brazil.
(stunned) A panther?
(emphatically) I'm not going to have Drusilla lording it over me any longer with her cheetah. Mark cabled me that he had the panther. I can't think why it hasn't arrived. Maybe he's training it.
(faintly) Excuse me. (staggers from the room in search of David)
Has she ever been clawed by a panther?
148 INT. GUEST BEDROOM - NIGHT
David is just hanging up the telephone receiver as Susan dashes into the room, skids on the rug and falls at his feet.
Don't call the Zoo! Don't call the Zoo, David!
I've called the Zoo. It's all fixed!
Call them back and unfix it! Don't ask questions! Call them back!
I will not! I told the Zoo they could have the panther if they found him.
You gave him Baby! You gave away my life's happiness -- my brother's trust in me! You gave away my inheritance!
I've had enough! I quit!
(thrusting receiver at him) You can't quit!
You told me the only thing standing between you and a million dollars was that panther. Well -- now it's gone!
(wailing) But, David, everything's changed. I just found out that panther belongs to Aunt Elizabeth! (dancing in a frenzy) I'll explain later! Call them back and tell them you were mistaken.
I've just spent fifteen minutes convincing them that I did see a panther. They won't believe me!
(wrenching receiver from him) I'll tell them you're a drug addict -- that you're always seeing things!
(wrenching receiver back) You'll do nothing of the sort! (starts to dial)
149 INT. VANCE DINING ROOM - NIGHT
Aunt Elizabeth and Major Applegate have reached the dessert and coffee.
I'd like to finance your expedition, Horace -- but there is something to be said for furthering science. Without science we wouldn't have plastic surgery and the telephone.
Without science, Elizabeth, we'd be free of this complicated civilization. The primitive life is the richer life. Men and women -untrammeled -- Think of it!

I also have to think of the responsibility of giving away so much money. I'd like to have my name in the Museum. But before I make up my mind I must talk to this young Dr. Huxley I've heard so much about. Then I'll be able to decide between you -- the Museum -- and Susan. I had my heart set on showing you the jungle, Elizabeth. Picture it! A
vast mysterious silence vibrant with life -- strange cries in the night There is a strange cry in the night, which we know is Baby but they don't. They listen. George gets up and goes to the window.
Did you hear that, Horace?
A loon! (resuming his rhapsody) Once you got the jungle in your blood, Elizabeth, I wouldn't be able to keep you out of it.
I'd have to come out every now and then for a permanent.
Picture bathing in a cool, limpid stream ---Again Baby screams. George drops his paws from the window and trots out.
150 INT. GUEST BEDROOM NIGHT
David hangs up the receiver, defeated.
There's nobody there. The night watchman says they've all gone out panther hunting.
Then there's only one thing to do, David. We must catch him before they do! What is the best thing to take when you're going to catch a panther.
(mad) A bigger panther!
151 INT. VANCE DINING ROOM - NIGHT
Aunt Elizabeth and Major Applegate are at coffee as Susan and David enter. David starts walking around the table, peering under it for George.
What are you looking for?
(agitated) George! Where's George?
I don't know! What does it matter?
Matter?... Susan, you've lost George again!
Why this morbid interest in George?
He has my intercostal clavicle!
Oh, Horace, take him away!
Don't be angry with David, Auntie. He's just overwrought.
I wish he'd go somewhere else and be overwrought!
They are diverted by the sudden outcry of the panther. Susan and David look at each other.
Horace! Are you sure that's a loon?
I didn't hear anything.
I've heard many a loon, Elizabeth, and if ever I heard a loon that's a loon. Isn't it, Mr. Bone?
No.
Well, what would you say it was?
(savagely) Well, since you put it that way I'd say it's a panther!
Susan just moans. David is on the loose.
Nonsense! Major Applegate would know a panther if he heard one!
Of course I would. I'm an authority on animal cries.
All right -- it's a loon! The panther cry is something you can never forget.
I know.
It varies at different seasons. Now -- let me see -- what month is this AUNT ELIZABETH
APPLEGATE
Oh yes, June. Now let me see. June. It would go something like this. He cups his hands and lets out an unearthly scream.
It's still a loon! I haven't practiced the panther cry in a long time. I'm slightly out of voice.
Carefully cups his hands, but just as he is about to cut loose Baby screams from near the house. Everybody looks at Horace as if he were a ventriloquist.
(with a moan) Ohhhhh!
(nervously) I didn't do that.
Try again, Major. Maybe it will come in and we'll see what it is!
It was probably an echo.
It was a long time coming back!

Applegate cups his hands a gain, glancing over his shoulder dubiously, then lets out a piercing yodel. All listen except Susan, who puts her fingers in her ears. Back comes the answering scream of Baby.
That's curious. There aren't any panthers in Connecticut, are there?
Yes.
Susan, seeing that David is mad enough to tell the whole story, thinks fast and frantically. She rises from the table weakly, her hands fluttering uncertainly, her eyes in a fixed, unnatural stare.
I feel funny.
(savagely) Well, you're not, you little fiend! You're just plain vicious!
Susan, what is it? I don't like your saying that my niece is vicious, Mr.
Bone. She has very kind instincts.
She has all the kind instincts of a cobra. Only a cobra doesn't go looking for you.
She's ill!
Susan is rocking back and forth, looking for a convenient place to fall. She reels about until there are no chairs in the way.
She's going to faint!
(faintly) I'll be all right.
You bet she'll be. All right! She'd be all right in a den of lions!
Susan totters gracefully and falls.
(running to Susan)
Hannah! Horace! Get me some water!
The smelling salts, Hannah!
She's shamming.
Applegate brings a carafe of water, tremblingly pours a glass and hands it to Aunt Elizabeth, who props up Susan's head and tries to force it between her lips.
Is she coming to, Elizabeth?
(despairingly) No.
Hannah enters with the smelling salts. Aunt Elizabeth wrenches them from her hand.
(calmly) If the little beast knew how silly she looked, she'd revive soon enough.
(sitting up; indignantly) Well, of all the heartless brutes!
Maybe. But I'm not a fool. I know when people faint they don't fall backward.
(getting to her feet with alacrity. Now she's mad)
All right, Mr. David Hux-DAVID (silencing her wildly by pushing motions through the air with his hands) No! No, Susan! What do you want me to do? Whatever it is, I'll do it.
(at her wits' ant patience' end) This is a madhouse! I need some air. Horace, take me for a walk in the garden!
(rising) Why, of course, Elizabeth. Nothing like a stroll after dinner to quiet the nerves. They start for the entry.
152 EXT. VANCE GARDEN - NIGHT
Aunt Elizabeth and Applegate are strolling in the moonlight. Patches of shrubbery throw deep shadows.
You're very quiet, Horace.
I can't understand why that loon should answer a panther cry. There's something wrong. I very rarely miss.
The urge is too much for him. He must prove to himself that he can cry like a panther. He croons it softly.
153 INT. VANCE KITCHEN - NIGHT

Mrs. Gogarty is washing dishes. Gogarty, at one side of the room, is busily cleaning his gun. We can see that he is cleaning his gun because that will take time and the more time it takes the further away the cat that he is going to hunt will be. Susan strolls in with an air of innocence.
I have a little headache. if Aunt Elizabeth asks for me, tell her that I've gone to bed. What's the gun for, Gogarty?
If you'd seen what I saw, Miss Susan, you'd be lookin' your windows and beggin' me on your knees to sit outside your bedroom door all night.

I -- I have an errand for you to do, Gogarty. There's a car out in the stable. I want you to take it into Westlake and leave it in front of the butcher shop -- later tonight. (walks around him speculatively) Are you a -- good marksman, Gogarty?
He can't hit the broad side of a barn.
There never was an Irishman yet, Hannah Gogarty, that didn't have the eye
of an eagle. And Aloysius Gogarty is no exception.
Guns are dangerous things, Gogarty. I shouldn't fool around with that one too much, if I were you. You might shoot somebody.
I'll shoot nobody. The responsibility of protectin' helpless women lies heavily on me shoulders. Nobody could ever say that Aloysius Gogarty wasn't on the alert.

You -- uh -- you won't forget about the car, will you, Gogarty? In front of the butcher shop, mind. (sets down the empty glass and starts out) And, Gogarty, don't be conspicuous about it. Just take it in quietly, get out quietly and catch the bus back at about -- oh, I should say -- at about midnight. (she drifts on out the door)
154 EXT. VANCE GARDEN - NIGHT
Applegate and Aunt Elizabeth strolling.
I don't know, Horace. A loon and a panther sound very much alike to me.
No, no, Elizabeth. A panther cry is like this!
And cupping his hands he lets out a fearful scream.
155 EXT. VANCE GROUNDS - NIGHT
Baby loping across a lawn toward the two figures. [Scene Details]
156 EXT. VANCE GARDEN - NIGHT
STROLLING SHOT of Applegate and Aunt Elizabeth as Baby glides in and slinks along contentedly behind them.
I learned it from a bushman in the Congo. It takes years of practice.
It's done largely with the palate. You curl the tongue so -(curls his tongue) Thus the sound comes up, and out. Also, the hands play an important part

throat -Again he rends the silence. At this moment they have passed some shrubbery and Baby, who has slunk into the black shadow, answers him enthusiastically. Baby's scream is better. Applegate whirls on Aunt Elizabeth.
Elizabeth! Not so fast! (as she stares at him) But I must say that's remarkably good. I think you've got it!
Got what?
The mating cry!
Don't be rude, Horace.
But you don't seem to realize what you've achieved.
Horace! What are you talking about?
I'm talking about the way you screamed.
I didn't scream.
(looking around uneasily) I'm beginning to think there's something wrong here.
His gaze fixes on something behind her. Baby standing in the clear moonlight. as Applegate reacts.
I -- think -- it's time -- we went back -- to the house.
We just left the house, Horace.
I Know -- but don't you think -- it would be a good idea -- If we went back?
No, I don't I think it would be a good idea if we continued our walk and stopped talking about panthers and screams.
But, Elizabeth -- don't you find it -- a bit chilly? He is walking along beside her, CAMERA FOLLOWING, peering over his shoulder.
Chilly? Nonsense! It's positively balmy!
(irritated) Who's balmy?
(stopping) Horace! What's come over you?
(pleadingly) Elizabeth -- I think we ought to go in.
But, Horace!, I don't want to go in, I like it here.

They start walking again, CAMERA FOLLOWING. Horace is still peering over his shoulder. He is aware that it's up to him as a man to conceal from Aunt Elizabeth the fact that Death is at this very moment stalking her. He is also aware that Aunt Elizabeth, when she wants to walk, will walk, come hell or high water. He propels her through the door and pushes in after her in frantic haste. CAMERA FOLLOWS them as they catapult into the entryway. Aunt Elizabeth is thoroughly bewildered. Applegate is thoroughly jittery.
Did I hear that man -- Gogarty- say that he -- had a gun?
I don't know whether you heard him say so, but he has.
Where will I find him?
In the kitchen, I should think. Why?
CAMERA FOLLOWS Applegate as he rushes away from her without a word, in the direction of the kitchen.
159 INT. VANCE KITCHEN - NIGHT
Gogarty is holding the gun up stock first, peering down the barrel. Mrs. Gogarty has finished the dishes and is rinsing out the tea towels.

...and so I said to her, I said, 'Mrs. Random, your niece is a nitwit!' And she just looked at me. 'Furthermore, Mrs. Random,' I said, 'if she wants to dig holes she can fill up the holes. It's not Gogarty that's going to do it.'
The kitchen door bursts open and Applegate rushes in. He snatches at the gun. Gogarty resists manfully but is forced to surrender it.
What did you say you saw?
(bristling) I said I saw a cat as big as a cow, and I saw a cat as big as a cow -- a small cow. And if you've come in here to insinuate -APPLEGATE Come with me, my good man!
Gogarty marches behind him.
160 INT. ENTRY, VANCE HOUSE - NIGHT
Aunt Elizabeth is wondering at Applegate's haste. Baby pads in, vanishing into dining room. Applegate comes dashing out of the kitchen, bearing the gun, Gogarty behind him.
(pointing at gun) What's that for?
I want you to promise me that you'll stay indoors -- till I come back. And keep the doors closed.
She watches him as he goes from window to window, closing and locking them.
Why are you doing that?
I don't know. I just have a feeling -- that this is the best thing to do.
We've got to keep everything out -- we must be sure that nothing gets in -AUNT ELIZABETH Including air?

Yes, The night air -- is very dangerous, Nothing must get in! Pantomiming the closing of windows, he motions Gogarty into the bedroom to do likewise. Intent on barricading the house he heads for the dining room, Aunt Elizabeth sinks into a chair.
Balmy! The lot of them!
161 INT. VANCE DINING ROOM - NIGHT

Baby pads around, looking for a place to curl up. He strolls behind a chair as Applegate enters and goes to a window which looks out on the garden. As he is bolting this window, Baby comes around the end of the table and goes over to rub against the backs of his legs. With a despairing moan Applegate trips and falls over the panther. Baby leaps lightly through the second window and vanishes. Gogarty enters the room, looks at the prone Applegate, closes and locks the open window. Applegate, scrambling to his feet, seizes the gun, turns and dashes out through the entry. Gogarty follows.
162 EXT. RIVERBANK - NIGHT
TRUCK SHOT Susan and David searching for Baby. Susan is singing, 'I Can't Give You Anything But Love, Baby.' She breaks off and turns on David, who is loaded down with the fish net and rope.
David, you might sing. At least.
I can't sing.
You can, too. You have a fine strong voice.
But not for singing.
It's not fair. You're just being stubborn! I'm doing all the work. I'll bet if Miss Swallow told you to you'd sing. I'll bet if Miss Swallow told you to do anything you'd do it.
(bitterly) I'd have to see her first. But I have a feeling I never will.
The bark of a dog is heard. David stops rigidly and looks across the river.
That's a dog!
(looking across) It's George! (squeals out as she points off) And Baby!
163 EXT. OPPOSITE BANK - NIGHT
WHAT THEY SEE: SHOT of the panther and dog approaching on a rise of ground, so that they show clearly in the moonlight. George is walking stiff-legged, sidling up toward the panther, which crouches.
164 EXT. RIVERBANK - NIGHT
Susan and David watching this.
(in a frozen tone) In another minute my intercostal clavicle will be gone. Forever, (putting his hand on her arm beseechingly) Susan, now think! Is there any way to cross this river?
Oh yes. It's only about so deep. (indicating a foot) We can wade across.
They run down the bank, Susan in the lead, CAMERA TRUCKING with them. She steps in the water and immediately disappears.
(with a yell as she comes up) The river bed's changed!
She goes down again. David jumps in and pulls her out. She coughs and chokes as he slaps her impatiently on the back.
(in a daze) Did we get across?
(bitterly) No, Susan we're right back where we started. Only we're wet.
Well, while you were about it, you might as well have gone to the other
side.
(savagely) While I'm wet I might as well go to the other side! (dives in and starts swimming across)
(squealing to him) David! Wait for me! (dives in after him)
165 EXT. OPPOSITE BANK - NIGHT
Susan and David emerge dripping from the water, climb the bank and look at the rise of ground. 166 WHAT THEY SEE George and Baby playing.
yell simultaneously.
BABY!
GEORGE!
168 BABY AND GEORGE
- playing. Startled by the cries, they cut and run, together.
169 SUSAN AND DAVID
turn on each other accusingly and speak simultaneously.
Now you've scared him away!
Did you have to yell like that?
170 EXT. FOOT OF DIRT BANK TEN FEET BELOW - NIGHT
Susan and David catapult into scene, land flat on their faces shoulder to shoulder. For a moment they lie with their faces down, then turn their heads and rest them on the earth, face to face.
(bitterly) Just because you're mad at me you didn't have to push me.
I regret to say, Susan, I didn't think of it.
(sighing) And it could be so nice. The moon and everything.... And we could have such fun. I like being with you, David.

Your powers of endurance are remarkable, Susan. Mine aren't so good. I like peace and quiet. (there is a long silence) You know, I've been thinking. It's foolish of me to be following you around, trying to find a panther that will bring you a million dollars I want for the Museum. (another thoughtful silence) If you'd planned it, you couldn't have ruined my chances for getting that bequest more thoroughly. You tell your aunt that I'm crazy. You tell her my name is Bone and you don't tell me. You tell her I'm a big game hunter and you don't tell me. You tell anybody anything that comes into your head and you don't tell me!
There's something else I forgot to tell you. Boopie's coming to the house tonight.
Slowly David rears up on his elbows and stares off into space, stunned.

If Peabody sees me, he's going to tell your aunt who I am. He thinks I'm crazy, too. He won't remember the four years I've spent on the brontosaurus group. He'll only remember that I hit him on the head with a rock -- he thinks.
It's quiet here, isn't it... Peaceful.
Yes. Let's stay here and let George and Baby look for us.
They lie in silence for a moment, both engrossed in their own thoughts -hers of love, his of murder. Faintly from the distance we hear the circus calliope.
(dreamily) Listen to the music... Where's it coming from?
That is Westlake. There's a circus there. Can you remember -- away back to this morning? That's where I'm supposed to be throwing knives -- and you're riding a trapeze. And here we are. Just loafing.
171 EXT. CIRCUS LOT - NIGHT

CLOSE UP SIGN, reading: SELLS-BAILY CIRCUS. The calliope is now loud. CAMERA PULLS BACK to bring into frame a cage-truck containing an enraged panther leaping against the bars. In b.g. is a suggestion of the Big Top -- gasoline flares, etc? Several roustabouts stand beside the cage.
They should of shot him last summer when he ripped up Caldoni.
Well, he sure gave that new trainer a going-over tonight!
I told 'em he'd do it again. Once a cat's bad, he's always bad. These jungle-bred cats are hard to handle, anyway.
(entering briskly) What are you waiting for, Joe?
(truck driver) Nobody's told me what to do with him, Boss.
If I had my way we'd shoot him right here. Baily says for you to take him into Bridgeport and put him in the gas chamber. (to roustabout) You go along with him, Mac.
Who? Me?
You heard what I said. (as Mac climbs in beside Joe) And keep your mouths shut. There's a lot of dough invested in these
animals, and we can't afford to have this kind of thing get out! Mac makes an okay gesture with his hand. The truck starts off.
172 EXT. HIGHWAY OUTSIDE TOWN - NIGHT
The cage-truck slows down and stops at a fork in the road. The panther is now quiet. In the moonlight we can see him silhouetted against the bars.
of Joe and Mac. They peer ahead.
(pointing right) I tell you Bridgeport's that way!
(pointing left) No, it ain't. It's that way! All right. Go that way. But you won't get to Bridgeport.
Where's that road map?
174 EXT. HIGH HEDGE AT ROADSIDE - NIGHT

Susan and David have built a small bonfire. Carefully laid out on rocks before it are David's shirt and Susan's dress. She is wearing his coat, which has already dried somewhat. He is clad only in his trousers.
If there'd been a bathhouse we could have done this much quicker.
Next time I'll try and arrange one. Or perhaps there are portable bathhouses for people like you. There must be.
Anyhow, I know how to build a fire without matches.
You probably had practice setting fire to the house when you were a child.
(through the hedge; he's mad and yelling now)
You see? It's that way!
You've got the map upside down!

Susan and David cautiously push through into the hedge and peer over. 175 WHAT THEY SEE The halted cage-truck directly before them. Silhouetted against the moon is the figure of the panther. The truck headlights illuminate the forked road. 176 CLOSE TWO SHOT Susan and David.
Look! The Zoo! They've got Baby!
Well, that's that. Is George with him?
I don't know! I can't see! But we have to get Baby away from them!
All I promised to do was help you find Baby. Well -- we've found him.
Susan begins to push David toward the front of the truck. CAMERA TRUCKS with them.
All you have to do is talk to those men! I'll do the rest!
But what can I talk about?
They're from the Zoo! Tell them you're a zoologist! Talk about zoos!
177 FRONT OF TRUCK
The truck just begins to move as David, sans shirt, steps into the glare of the headlights with upraised hand. Joe puts on the brake again.
Good evening!
Do you know the way to Bridgeport?
I'm not going to Bridgeport.
(tapping himself on the chest) We're going to Bridgeport. Do you know which way it is?
Yes.
They wait for him to go on, but he doesn't.
(pointing left) It's that way, ain't it?
Yes.
(pointing right) But I say it's that way.
David considers for a moment, gravely.
Yes. It is.
(protesting) But you said it was that way.

Gentlemen, if you doubt me why do you ask me? (this holds them for a moment) You can go that way -(pointing) or you can go this way -(pointing) but, as a matter of fact, I wouldn't advise either way. Why you should go to Bridgeport when the quickest way to New York is straight ahead, I don't know.
Joe and Mac. For a moment they are stunned.
This guy ain't got all his buttons.
Susan struggling with the hasp.
The poor li'l Baby. Did they lock him all up? Nassy ol' mans. Well, we'll fix that. (as panther strikes at her) Stop that, you fool -- or I'll smack you!
She swings the door open. The panther leaps out beside her, stands lashing his tail. She picks up the rope and approaches him coaxingly.
Now wait till Mummy gets his collar on.
The panther springs past her and over the hedge.
Baby! Baby! BABY! (indignantly) Oh, you brute!
180 FRONT OF TRUCK
David is still blocking the way, being completely unhelpful.

Now, Bridgeport is a nice little town, but with a population of fifty thousand you can't expect gaiety. New York, on the other hand, with seven million people, naturally tends to have centers of entertainment. All the best talent quite reasonably flows to a point where there will be more people to appreciate it.
Susan flies around and clutches at David in the glare of the lights. She is dragging the rope and looks equally nutty.
Ba-by!
Should we help you tie him up, lady?
No -- thank you. Just go away -- Everything will be quite -- all right.
181 CLOSE SHOT
Joe and Mac in the truck as Joe puts the truck in gear and starts forward on his own left turn. Mac leans out, looking after Susan and David.
Imagine being married to a guy like that! Poor kid! She's a neat number. as truck moves off out of scene.
183 EXT. AT TREE - NIGHT

The bad panther is climbing a tree. The next moment we hear Susan's voice, calling sweetly, "Baby! Baby!" She and David trudge in and stop directly under the panther, who is on a limb. They are now fully dressed. Susan points.
He went that way.
They are arrested by the cry of a panther -- which is Applegate. The panther above them lifts his head.
That's Baby!
He couldn't have got that far.
Oh, they can run very fast. The cry comes wafting down the wind once more.
184 EXT. OPEN MEADOW - NIGHT
Applegate, with Gogarty, comes wading through knee-deep grass. Gogarty bears the gun. Applegate stops, cups his hands and cries like a panther. Gogarty looks on in pleased admiration.
That's a noble noise, that is.
185 EXT. AT TREE - NIGHT
Susan, David and the panther above their heads, listening intently. Susan points off excitedly.
There he is again! Come on!
She plunges off, followed by David.
186 EXT. MEADOW - NIGHT
MED. SHOT -- Baby and George playing together. Applegate's luring call is heard. Baby scrambles to his feet and listens.
187 EXT. STONE WALL - NIGHT

Applegate, followed by Gogarty, climbs the wall. Finding the wall a point of vantage, he cups his hand and gives vent to the best cry of his career. Then both he and Gogarty listen intently. Back on the night air comes an answering cry. Applegate points to one side triumphantly. From the opposite direction an even better cry is heard. Instinctively he whirls and points in that direction. Gogarty, in a rage, throws the gun down. The place is lousy with panthers! DISSOLVE
188 EXT. THICKET - NIGHT

TRUCK SHOT -- Susan and David pushing their way through. As she parts the springy bushes one by one they fly back in David's face. Susan lets go a particularly strong limb and it gives him a terrific belt.
Ba-by! BA-by!... I've sort of lost my sense of direction. I wonder where we are?
My rough guess is somewhere near the Canadian border.
The last cry came from about here.
She trips over a vine and falls with a crash and a moan. David lifts her to her feet and sets her down with a jar.
Watch where you're going!
Watch where I'm going yourself, you smart Alec!
Now, Susan, you're tired and I'm tired. I'd suggest that we go home.
Go home yourself, Quitter! Go on home! The idea, turning a wild animal
loose on an innocent countryside and then going home and sleeping -- with lives in danger all over -DAVID Oh, Susan, do stop making a fool of yourself!
So!
She stalks off. David follows. CAMERA FOLLOWS them. Susan stops and faces David.
Please, David, I'd rather you didn't.
Didn't what?

Didn't come with me. (injured) After all I've gone through for you, that you can't come out and do a simple thing like helping me find a panther -- I think it's pretty shabby! I'd rather -- if you don't mind -- go on alone.
Oh, very well.

He sits down on a log and lights a cigarette. Susan pushes off through the brush. Her blind crashing is heard. There is a terrific crash, then silence. David listens as a gurgling cry rises to a wail. He calmly tamps out his cigarette and starts in the direction of the wailing.
189 EXT. IN BRUSH - NIGHT

Susan crying and rolling over as she tries to extricate herself from a mass of vines. David stalks in. Once more he lifts her to her feet. Now that she is no longer the managing Susan his tenderness comes to the fore. He still knows that she is a menace but he realizes that there is something soft and feminine in his arms.
Susan, your natural instinct for being wrong astounds me. But somehow I'd rather be with you wrong than with anyone else right.
Oh, Da-vid!
(mopping her tears) Your face is dirty.
(her mouth up) I d-don't c-care.

David is gradually growing less interested in mopping her face than in kissing it. He struggles manfully against the impulse but Susan lifts her face higher. Just as he is about to kiss her there is the unearthly scream of a panther, only a few yards away. David jumps and turns.
(dreamily) Don't bother, David.
But it's Baby!
Of course it is. What of it? (opens her eyes; he is gone) Oh, blast Baby! I'll make him pay for this!
She starts after David.
190 EXT. CLEARING - NIGHT
Baby and George come prowling along. David comes dashing out of the thicket, followed by Susan, who is now pursuing David, not the panther. David reaches Baby and begins to stroke him.
Good Baby. Nice Baby. (George growls; David looks at the dog grimly) I'll deal with you later. (reaches toward Susan, behind him)
Quick -- give me that rope!
I -- I lost it!
David, in dull fury, stares straight ahead.
All right. You take him home!
Come, Baby. Come home with Susan. Come, darling. Come, sweet.
Baby, to her astonishment, begins following her. CAMERA MOVES on them.
191 EXT. THICKET - NIGHT
Applegate stands all poised for the mating call, Gogarty behind him. Applegate cuts loose. Gogarty twitches at his sleeve.
How do you make that noise?
(impatiently) You couldn't learn it. It takes years of practice.
(defiantly) I could learn it in a week!
He cups his hands and makes an awful bawling sound. Applegate does a panther scream once more.
192 EXT. CLEARING - NIGHT
Susan and David leading panther and George. Applegate's scream rings hideously. Baby jumps and takes refuge in the nearest tree.
(slowly) That would be Applegate.
(bitterly as he eyes the panther hanging over a limb) Loon hunting, no doubt.
Don't you pay any attention to him, Baby. You come down to Susan. Susan will take care of you.
I suppose now you want me to climb up and push him down?
No. Shouldn't we try singing?
193 EXT. THICKET - NIGHT
Applegate, followed by Gogarty, claws his way through the thicket. Gogarty cups his hands and Applegate slaps his hands down.
Stop that! You'll have the whole countryside aroused!
194 EXT. CLEARING - NIGHT

Susan and David under the treed panther. Susan is singing, well into 'I Can't Give You Anything But Love, Baby'. George sits beside them, his feeling for music beginning to stir. David is picking up the song wherever he can find a word that he knows. George, caught by the spirit of the thing, lifts his muzzle and croons in unison -- he thinks. As the chorus rises Baby lifts his voice in his own version.
195 EXT. EDGE OF CLEARING - NIGHT
Applegate and Gogarty emerge from the shrubbery. Applegate stops and stares.
196 EXT. CLEARING - NIGHT
WHAT HE SEES: MED. LONG SHOT of the choral scene. To Applegate the panther seems to be crouching, preparatory to springing on Susan, David and George.
197 EXT. EDGE OF CLEARING - NIGHT
TWO SHOT -- Applegate and Gogarty. Applegate reaches for the gun, which Gogarty has already leveled and aimed, with a look of gladness in his eye.
Give me that! This has to be a dead shot!
He calls out to Susan and David encouragingly as Gogarty wrestles with him.
Don't move! Be quite calm! I'm here!
As Applegate wins the gun Gogarty speaks, a little mournfully.
I'm a dead shot.
198 EXT. CLEARING - NIGHT
Susan, David, George and the panther. The singing ceases abruptly.
Don't shoot! That's Baby! Stop him, David!
199 EXT. EDGE OF CLEARING - NIGHT

Applegate aiming. Gogarty, his head tilted on one side, is shaking it ominously. He knows that Applegate can't hit that panther. Applegate pulls the trigger. There is a hollow click. For the first time Applegate loses his head. He shakes the gun in front of Gogarty's face, hysterically.
Where are the cartridges?
(triumphantly and with an air of superiority) In the magazine, of course.
Applegate jerks the breech to pump a shell into the gun and as the shell goes in it also goes off. There is a splitting explosion.
200 EXT. CLEARING - NIGHT

Susan, David, George and Baby. At the report Baby leaps from the limb and streaks away. David makes a flying tackle at George and comes up with an armful of leaves. George too vanishes. Applegate, followed by Gogarty, comes dashing in.
Susan! Mr. Bone! Are you all right?
Oh, we're fine. We're just dandy.
(eyeing Susan and David with suspicion)
Now if Aloysius Gogarty had been wielding that gun you'd have seen a dead panther.
(to Applegate) Oh, why did you have to do that?
I'm sorry, Susan. I wouldn't have missed if my gun hadn't jammed.
Gogarty sneers and looks up at the stars.
(savagely) Major Applegate, we've spent the entire evening treeing that panther. It's tame -- don't you understand? It's a tame panther!

You, sir -- as everybody except you and Miss Vance seem to know -- are crazy. (turns to Susan) I forbid you to go further with this man. The panther is the most treacherous of animals. I will continue the hunt, but you must go home.
(in an undertone, twitching at his sleeve)
It's just as well. He couldn't be any crazier than she is.
Susan, don't you think that if we stopped trying to keep everything a secret from everybody there might be less confusion and more chance of getting the panther?
Do you think so?
I do. (turns to Applegate) You may have heard, sir, that Mrs. Random was expecting a panther from Brazil -- a pet panther. (points off) That is the panther.
I quit! Yes, sir, be gob, I quit! Aloysius Gogarty is goin' to curry no panther.
I don't believe it!
(twitching at his sleeve) I do.
I know it's hard to believe, but I can explain it.
Gogarty sneers again and looks up at the stars.
(to Applegate) If I told Aunt Elizabeth that you shot at her panther, where do you think you'd get with your old expedition? Applegate, whipsawed, looks from one to the other.
The only thing to do, Susan, is to explain things. (to Applegate) It all started this morning when Susan telephoned me...
201 EXT. VANCE FRONT PORCH - NIGHT

An open car stands in front of the porch. There are three men with guns in it, and a fourth man -- the same Zoo official we saw on the telephone with David -- is confronting Aunt Elizabeth, who is in the open doorway.
Lady, I'm trying to tell you we're from the Zoo. We had a telephone call. It came from here. This house!
But there must be some mistake. The panther hasn't come yet. Do you expect it to walk up -- and in?
Naturally not! I expect to receive a notice from the Freight Office first!
The Zoo Official looks at her and backs away.
(pacifyingly) Yes, lady. Don't get excited. Sure, you'll have a notice from the Freight Office.
Obviously, my good man, what you're looking for is a loon. (and she slams the door)
(yelling back)
I don't have to look any farther!
202 AT OPEN CAR
as the Zoo Official comes back to his friends. ZOO OFFICIAL Screwy!
203 EXT. CLEARING - NIGHT
GROUP SHOT on David, Susan, Major Applegate and Gogarty. Applegate and Gogarty are listening intently.
Well, sir, that's the story. We had him until you fired. Now the thing is to find him again.
You're quite sure he's -- harmless?
Oh, absolutely!
(twitching at Applegate's sleeve) I -- I patted him. I reached my hand out and patted him right on the
back. He's gentle as a kitten.

(to Susan and David) The practical thing is to go in different directions. He can't be far. (to David) You stay with Susan. If you lose your way, halloo. I'll come for you. (already lost, he looks around uncertainly) Now let's see... Which way did he go?
(pointing)
That way.
He's trying to throw us off.
And he starts off in the opposite direction, followed by Gogarty, who is suddenly very brave. Applegate stops and raises his finger admonishingly to Susan and David.
Don't forget now! If you get lost, halloo!
He vanishes. Susan and David have no choice but to take the other and proper direction. They start out.
204 EXT. OPEN MEADOW - NIGHT

Applegate and Gogarty emerge from the bushes. Gogarty is bearing the gun. Applegate peers off intently to left. Gogarty taps him on the shoulder and points off to right. 205 WHAT THEY SEE The bad panther stalking along in the clear moonlight. Gogarty and Applegate. Gogarty turns around without a word and starts in the opposite direction. Applegate follows.
There is nothing -- to be worried about. You -- just stand here. I'll take care of this.
(doubtfully) I just remembered an errand Miss Susan wanted me to do. Maybe I'd better be gettin' about it.
You -- say you -- patted -- this beast?
Well -- in a manner of speaking I -- yes, I patted him -- in a manner of

speaking. Yes, I patted him. But sometimes the dispositions of those -things are subject to -- a quick change. Applegate moves out of scene as Gogarty stands poised for flight. Gogarty draws a bottle from his hip pocket and takes a healthy drag on it. 207 MED. MOVING SHOT Panther as it prowls along a rise of ground, stops and looks past CAMERA, its eyes savage. The approaching figure of Applegate. Applegate snaps his fingers as he advances.
APPLEGATE
Come, Baby! Come, Kitty! Come, Puss! Kitty, kitty, kitty!
209 CLOSE SHOT
Panther as it snarls and lays back its ears. Applegate advancing. Suddenly he stops, doubtful.
Kitty?

The panther snarls and lunges forward. For a hurt and astonished instant Applegate stands his ground. Then we TRUCK with him as he turns and races back toward Gogarty, who is already under way. At this point the gun has become only an impediment to Gogarty, whose urgency to perform Susan's errand has been too much for him. He is loping without regard for Applegate or Applegate's safety, in the direction of the nearest thicket of trees. Applegate overhauls him. Gogarty trips and falls on his face, and the gun goes off. Applegate skids into Gogarty, grabs the gun and pumps another shell into it, levels the gun in the direction from which he has run.
211 REVERSE ANGLE
Bad panther, frightened by the explosion, bounding over the rise of ground and vanishing.
212 TWO SHOT
as Applegate breathes for the first time. Gogarty is painfully regaining his feet, attempting to find a reason for his lack of bravery.
I thought -- if I ran -- over there -- I'd get a -- better shot -- at him. Those -- those -- er -- that grass -- is a little -- high. I was making -- for that rise over here.
(bitterly) 'Gentle as a kitten!'
Gogarty silently hands his bottle to Applegate. Applegate uncorks it and drinks.
213 TRUCK SHOT
on Susan and David trudging along country road.
You know, David -- I've been trying to figure out why you didn't kiss me back there. You almost did.
Susan, I think we won't discuss that.

But, David, there's -- nothing really wrong in kissing a girl. I mean, it's just that you have to get a point of view about it. If, for instance, you were to touch my face -- this way -(reaches out and pats him) -- there wouldn't be anything wrong about that, would there? You could even do it accidentally, couldn't you?
I couldn't kiss you accidentally, Susan. If I kissed you it would be quite deliberate.
(her brand of logic) Well -- there wouldn't be anything wrong in your reaching out and
touching my cheek deliberately, would there?
No. I suppose there wouldn't.
Then why would it be wrong for you to kiss me, deliberately? It's not -much different, really -- is it, David?
Yes. It's quite different.
Well, I'd like -- I'd like -- to try it and see -- why it's different. I don't see any difference.
Susan, I am an engaged man. If I hadn't lost my fossil, I would be a married man.
(quickly) Oh, I wouldn't kiss a married man.
She has edged up to him and is walking so close that she all but trips him as she stares up hungrily into his face.
I don't intend to kiss you, Susan. You might as well put it out of your pretty little head. I intend to go back to New York and be married.
They are passing a country house, when a low growl is heard. They stop and stare off.
What's that?
There is another growl.
(simultaneously) George!
They dive in the direction of the sound.
214 EXT. DIGBY'S LAWN - NIGHT
George on lawn in front of house. Susan and David come around a lilac bush and bear down on him purposefully! George backs off. They follow. Suddenly Susan points up, her voice husky with excitement.
Baby!
215 EXT. DIGBY VERANDA ROOF - NIGHT
WHAT THEY SEE: Baby outlined against the sky, on the veranda roof.
216 EXT. DIGBY LAWN - NIGHT

Susan, David and George. Susan stands still, looking upward at Baby, while David pursues the one being that knows where his intercostal clavicle is. George trots off around the house. David follows. Susan advances toward veranda.
217 EXT. SIDE OF DIGBY HOUSE - NIGHT

as Joe leans out further, nettled.

(cups his hands and bawls) Halloooo!

CLOSE SHOT Mrs. Digby, now in a wrapper, on the telephone

Dr. Digby has Susan in a chair under a strong light and is in the midst of a psychiatric examination.

pursuing the young man. Isn't that so?

quickly the first thing that comes into your head. You understand?

(springing a trap) Panther!
George!

Oh, don't be silly!

Baby!

David!
David!
George!
(growing more frantic) George!
Baby!
Baby!
George!
George!
Intercostal clavicle!

silhouetted against the study window. Past him the constables enter and grab him. There is a struggle. George escapes.

David!
(wearily) George!
George!
Intercostal clavicle!
Intercostal clavicle!
(dragged in by constables) Where?
(points a quivering finger at David) YOU!
(mechanically) David!

MED. SHOT -- Susan and David behind the bars of adjoining cells with a barred partition between them. David stands dejectedly holding a handkerchief over one eye. Susan is on her knees in a busy attempt to pick the lock of her cell with a hairpin.
Susan, what are you doing?

Don't be a fool.

(rising stiffly) Madam, we'll pardon you because you are obviously hysterical. Now what do you want?

(shouting)

(simultaneously; slightly impeded by his gun) Gentlemen -- gentlemen -- please! There is no need for violence. This can all be settled quietly!
From the cells come Susan's howls.

(trying to hold the gun out of reach; in an anguished voice)
(screaming) Idiots! Nitwits! Stop it! Do you hear me? Stop it!

the Zoo official and Joe, the driver of the cage-truck, lean toward each other, speaking simultaneously.

Slocum and Elmer are outside David's cell with Dr. Digby behind them, taking notes. We see Susan sitting on the floor, her elbows on her knees, her two hands cupping her face, as she listens. Another of Susan's horrible plans is forming. David leans against the bars dejectedly.

The bad panther is striking and snarling viciously through the steel bars at Baby and George, who cower in opposite corners at the back, looking appealingly at Aunt Elizabeth for protection. David enters with the chair before him, closing in on the bad panther, which turns and faces him with a snarl, backing up. David yells frenziedly:

The panther backs slowly, menacingly in through the open door of the last vacant cell and David bangs the door on it just as the panther springs. In a state of collapse, not knowing what he is doing, he sinks weakly into the chair which he has been carrying. The panther springs ferociously at the bars behind him. But David no longer knows nor cares. As he sits there, Susan comes crawling in to him on her knees. This act of heroism has been too much for her. She puts her arms around his waist as she thrills with love.
Da-vid! Are you all right?
Her voice rouses him even more than the snarling of the panther behind him. He rises uncertainly to his feet.

No! I'm not all right I'll never be all right again! Susan Vance, you're a menace to society. I never want to see you or your aunt or your panther again! I'm going back to my Museum, and I'm going to be quiet! (hysterically) Quiet, quiet, do you hear? Did you ever hear the word?
And he staggers out, leaving her still on her knees with supplicating arms reaching after him. She turns on the bad panther in a reproachful wail.

David, more worried about Susan than about his brontosaurus slides down the ladder and begins excavating.
